skill.name: spec-driven-development
skill.description: Guides AI-assisted software work through a seven-phase Spec-Driven Development workflow with governance, clarification, planning, analysis, task breakdown, and implementation checks.
skill.summary: Use a governed Spec-Driven Development workflow to turn feature ideas into validated plans, tasks, and implementations.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command, PowerShell, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found they are markdown artifact paths, methodology examples, or script directory references. No executable code, network access, credential access, prompt injection attempt, obfuscated payload, or malicious intent was found in SKILL.md. The remaining risk is low because the workflow can ask an AI agent to create project files and automation scripts, so users should review generated artifacts before execution.

low_findings.title: Static command findings are documentation references
low_findings.description: The reported backtick and PowerShell matches occur in markdown paths, code-fenced examples, and directory listings. They do not contain executable commands, shell interpolation, downloaded payloads, or instructions to run untrusted code directly.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched text is visible markdown documentation and example output, not runnable source code. The surrounding context describes artifacts and folder structure for a development method.

low_findings.title: Workflow may generate files and automation scripts
low_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to set up project structure, create specification artifacts, and generate bash or PowerShell script variants. This is legitimate for a development workflow, but users should inspect generated scripts and file changes before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow clearly includes file and script generation, which can affect a repository. No malicious content is present, so the risk is operational rather than hostile.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts are false positives
low_findings.description: The high-risk weak cryptography alerts map to ordinary prose and artifact names, while reconnaissance alerts map to best-practice and scenario text. No hashing code, cryptographic API usage, host enumeration, or system data collection is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain methodology language, headings, or scenario descriptions. There is no evidence of cryptographic implementation or system reconnaissance behavior.

content.value_statement: AI-assisted projects often fail when requirements, plans, and tasks drift apart. This skill provides a phased workflow that keeps specifications, governance, analysis, and implementation aligned.
actual_capabilities: Defines a seven-phase workflow from project initialization through implementation validation.
Guides creation of specification, plan, research, data model, contract, and task artifacts.
Applies constitutional governance principles to keep decisions consistent across phases.
Structures user stories and tasks for independent testing and parallel work.
Adds quality gates for clarification, analysis, checklist validation, and completion review.
Supports coordination across Claude, Codex, Claude Code, and other AI coding agents.
limitations: It is a methodology guide, not a complete project generator by itself.
Generated scripts and implementation changes still require human review before execution.
It works best when users can define clear business goals and acceptance criteria.
It may add process overhead for very small or throwaway changes.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Product Feature
use_cases.description: Turn an idea into stakeholder-focused requirements, clarified assumptions, technical planning, and implementation-ready tasks.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Multiple AI Agents
use_cases.description: Use shared artifacts, phase gates, and task grouping to divide work across Claude, Codex, Claude Code, and other tools.

use_cases.title: Standardize Governed Delivery
use_cases.description: Create a repeatable workflow with constitution rules, quality gates, analysis checks, and measurable success criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Feature Spec
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use spec-driven-development to create a feature specification for this idea: [describe feature]. Focus on user value, user stories, acceptance scenarios, edge cases, and measurable success criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Clarify Requirements
prompt_templates.prompt: Use spec-driven-development to review this specification for unclear or missing decisions. Ask up to five high-impact clarification questions, one question at a time.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Implementation Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Move from specification to technical design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use spec-driven-development to produce a technical plan from the approved specification. Include research questions, architecture choices, data model needs, contracts, and project structure.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze and Break Down Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Use spec-driven-development to analyze the specification, plan, and tasks for conflicts or gaps. Then create dependency-ordered tasks grouped by independently testable user stories.

output_examples.input: A team wants to add account recovery to an existing application.
output: A stakeholder-focused feature specification with prioritized recovery scenarios.
Clarification questions for identity verification, expiry rules, and security requirements.
A task breakdown covering tests, service changes, user interface updates, and validation.

output_examples.input: An engineering lead wants multiple agents to implement a payment feature safely.
output: A constitution-aligned plan with integration constraints and quality gates.
Parallel user story groups with explicit dependency boundaries.
An analysis report that flags ambiguity, duplication, and missing acceptance coverage.

output_examples.input: A prototype needs clear requirements before coding starts.
output: A lightweight specification that separates business goals from implementation details.
Measurable success criteria that can be checked after implementation.
A focused list of implementation tasks for the first usable slice.

best_practices: Complete clarification before planning so important decisions are not hidden in implementation work.
Keep specifications focused on user value and measurable outcomes, not internal design details.
Review generated scripts, file changes, and task plans before running or assigning implementation work.
anti_patterns: Starting implementation before the specification and success criteria are clear.
Using parallel agents without dependency tracking or shared artifact formats.
Treating generated plans as final without checking constitution alignment and test coverage.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides AI-assisted development through specification, clarification, planning, analysis, task breakdown, and implementation phases.

faq.question: Is this only for new projects?
faq.answer: No. It can guide new projects, large features, governed changes, and complex refactors that need clear planning.

faq.question: Does it write production code automatically?
faq.answer: It can guide implementation tasks, but users should review generated code, scripts, and repository changes before use.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code. The methodology also discusses multi-agent coordination.

faq.question: When is this workflow too heavy?
faq.answer: It may be excessive for small edits, quick experiments, or tasks with clear requirements and low coordination needs.

faq.question: How does it improve team coordination?
faq.answer: It uses shared artifacts, phase gates, user story grouping, and dependency tracking to reduce ambiguity across contributors.
